name: RawData+opencog-ml+Building minecraft bot
content:
Source: opencog-dev mailing list
Thread: Building minecraft bot
Date range: Sat, 5 Dec 2015 07:22:59 -0800 (PST) to Tue, 12 Jan 2016 18:57:23 -0800 (PST)
Messages: 54
Participants: Andrew Buck, Linas Vepstas, Roman Treutlein, Yi-Shan Chen, Fredy, Ben Goertzel, Fredy Villa, Amen Belayneh, mandeep bhatia, Akhilesh Sudhakar

From: Andrew Buck
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 2015 07:22:59 -0800 (PST)

Hello everyone,

Over the last couple days I have been working on getting the 
opencog-to-minecraft bot based on ROS set up and ready to develop on.  I 
think I am almost there, having installed opencog, ROS, and the bot.  I got 
to the point where I am actually trying to run [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 2015 12:02:16 -0600

Well, spock seems to be here:
https://github.com/SpockBotMC/SpockBot

and a quick look at the commit graphs shows is been very active with lots
of things changing.

So I type in "BoundBuffer" in the "search this repository" box and get this:

https://github.com/SpockBotMC/SpockBot/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=BoundBuffer

For PluginLoader I get this:
https://github.com/SpockBotMC/SpockBot/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=PluginLoader&type=Code

If you're lucky, maybe just changing the imports is enough.

-- Linas

with getting the opencog bot to run by changing the import statements and

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 2015 22:20:21 -0600

Looks like you're working with the wrong repo; you should be working with
https://github.com/opencog/opencog-to-minecraft/pulls  and not the OC2MC
one ...

On Sat, Dec 5, 2015 at 10:18 PM, Andrew Buck <andrew.r.buck@gmail.com>
wrote:

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 2015 22:27:12 -0600

And the punishment for working with the wrong repo is that you haven't
gotten the lastest fixes, e.g. the spockbot fixes here:

https://github.com/opencog/opencog-to-minecraft/commit/de4fc0839b56965682499b47ae5a9e60b5c099d0

One way to observe that you might be working with an out-of-date repo is to
always start by exploring the "network graph" --click on "graphs" then
click "network".  This will show you who is doing what, and lets you poke
around to see what's going on.  In this case, you should have spotted that
the opencog repo is newer than the OC2MC repo.

Whenever starting work on a new github project, this is a must-do work
item. You can easily discover dead projects this way, as well as vibrant
ones.  And then, once you are working, check peridocially, because shit
comes out of [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sat, 5 Dec 2015 23:35:45 -0600

Heh. OK. so, if you look at the github page, in the upper right, you see
the word "fork" and a number next to it. The number next to it is the
number of repositories on github for "this same code."  So, for
https://github.com/opencog/opencog its 355 -- lots and lots of copies of
almost the same code.

Doesn't mean much; means that 355 people clicked on the "fork" button. Most
probably never made changes, but some did. Many then made pull requests to
merge thier changes, but many did not.

Git is peer-to-peer.  There's no single "master", but there is a copy that
tends to be the best-maintained, most-actively maintained, and for opencog,
its here: https://github.com/opencog

Use search to find any/all wikipages that pertain to it...

--linas

On Sat, Dec 5, 2015 at 11:01 PM, Andrew Buck [...]

From: Andrew Buck
Date: Sun, 6 Dec 2015 05:32:27 -0800 (PST)

Yeah, I know how forks work, apparently I just got mixed up somewhere and 
ended up forking from a one which was behind the main line.  Would be nice 
if there was a bit more prominent warning when something like this happens 
but in any case I have it all sorted out now.  As a [...]

---

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Sun, 6 Dec 2015 10:12:36 -0600

Since all forks are peers, you don't have to delete a fork just to change
where pulls come from or where pushes go.  This is enough:

git remote set-url origin git@github.com://blah/blah   for pulls
git remote set-url --push origin git@github.com://yourname/blah

you can even set it up so you pull from multiple locations at once, or push
to them.

As a peer-to-peer network, the above just controls which nodes in the
network you are connected to.  You can connect to any nodes at all.  Even
nodes that were not forked from the same repo, even those that contain
something completely different (well, that would be completely weird, but
it would still work)

--linas

From: Ben Goertzel
Date: Sun, 27 Dec 2015 00:31:40 +0800

On Sun, Dec 27, 2015 at 12:24 AM, Linas Vepstas <linasvepstas@gmail.com>
wrote:

Shujing's current goal is to finish her PhD thesis ;p

Her  main thesis topic is the OpenCog pattern miner.  But as part of her
thesis, she wants to run the pattern miner on an Atomspace generated from
game-play in some video game world....

She judged it would be faster to

A) generate such an Atomspace by tweaking her old planner on the Unity3D
game world

rather than

B) generate it by porting her planner to the Minecraft world

because her planner relied on a whole lot of stuff from the old Embodiment
module, which has been removed now...

She understands that A is requiring her to work on throwaway code, but in
this context she's just aiming to get some interesting research results for
her thesis not [...]

From: Linas Vepstas
Date: Mon, 28 Dec 2015 20:12:00 -0600

If it was transferred, there are two choices: rename it to "deprecated" in
some obvious way, or just delete it. Either requires more work, and there
doesn't seem to be any point to it, other than to unconfuse google.   I
figure after some months, google will figure it out on its own, eventually,
without us trying to figure out how to trick it.  Its how git was designed
to work; I see no harm in letting things take their natural course.

--linas

the entities (exp orb, dropped item, zombie, etc).  [...]
